defs: Originally, a covered porch with seats, at a house door; the Dutch stoep as introduced by the Dutch into New York. Afterward, an out-of-door flight of stairs of from seven to fourteen steps, with platform and parapets, leading to an entrance door some distance above the street; the French perron. Hence, any porch, platform, entrance stairway, or small veranda, at a house door.

records.ety: [OE. stope, Icel. staup; akin to AS. step, D. stoop, G. stauf, OHG. stouph.]

defs: A vessel of liquor; a flagon.
quotes: Fetch me a stoop of liquor.

defs: A post fixed in the earth.

records.ety: [OE. stoupen; akin to AS. st<?/pian, OD. stuypen, Icel. stūpa, Sw. stupa to fall, to tilt. Cf 5th Steep.]
records.morph: [imp. & p. p. Stooped (?); p. pr. & vb. n. Stooping.]

defs: To bend the upper part of the body downward and forward; to bend or lean forward; to incline forward in standing or walking; to assume habitually a bent position.
To yield; to submit; to bend, as by compulsion; to assume a position of humility or subjection.
quotes: Mighty in her ships stood Carthage long, . . . Yet stooped to Rome, less wealthy, but more strong.
These are arts, my prince, In which your Zama does not stoop to Rome.

defs: To descend from rank or dignity; to condescend.
quotes: Where men of great wealth stoop to husbandry, it multiplieth riches exceedingly.

defs: To come down as a hawk does on its prey; to pounce; to souse; to swoop.
quotes: The bird of Jove, stooped from his aery tour, Two birds of gayest plume before him drove.

defs: To sink when on the wing; to alight.
quotes: And stoop with closing pinions from above.
Cowering low With blandishment, each bird stooped on his wing.

defs: To bend forward and downward; to bow down; as, to stoop the body.
To cause to incline downward; to slant; as, to stoop a cask of liquor.
To cause to submit; to prostrate.
quotes: Many of those whose states so tempt thine ears Are stooped by death; and many left alive.

defs: The act of stooping, or bending the body forward; inclination forward; also, an habitual bend of the back and shoulders.
Descent, as from dignity or superiority; condescension; an act or position of humiliation.
quotes: Can any loyal subject see With patience such a stoop from sovereignty?

defs: The fall of a bird on its prey; a swoop.
